schools.summary: No pitched battles but a war of rival claims; time and the nation's growing confidence steadily transferred strength from Saul's house to David's.
Gill fixes the chronology: the war ran five years more until Ish-bosheth's death, when David had reigned above seven years in Hebron.
Henry: God prolonged the conflict to try David's faith; the long war mirrors the believer's struggle of grace against corruption.
The 'long war' was five years of skirmishes; David's house strengthened as Saul's declined toward Ish-bosheth's murder.
The birth register is not a digression but documentary proof of the strengthening house, following the custom of opening a reign with family notices.
Gill: David had wives in exile but no sons there; the fruitfulness of Hebron marks the change in his fortunes.
Henry: each son was a fresh security to the commonwealth, yet the several wives were a fault against the law.
Gill reconciles Chileab with the Chronicler's 'Daniel' ('God hath judged me' against Nabal) and reads 'Chileab' as 'like his father.'
Wesley reads David's marriage to Maacah of Geshur as policy for a northern ally, for which he 'paid dear' in Absalom.
Keil keeps the register in order before turning at the sixth son to the decline of Saul's house and Abner's power.
Gill reads Adonijah forward: the son now counted among David's strength would usurp the throne and die by Solomon's order.
Keil keeps the list in place, naming each son and mother as part of the family notice documenting David's rise.
Henry: rejoice with trembling in building up families, for three of these six sons came to infamy.

themes: the perils of a large household

schools.summary: Gill canvasses why Eglah alone is called 'David's wife' and rejects the rabbinic identification of her with Michal.
Wesley inclines to the Michal reading and draws the lesson that six sons by six wives brought less honour than one wife might.

debates.question: Why is Eglah alone called 'David's wife,' and is she Michal?

sides.position: a woman of no note, distinguished only by relation to David; not Michal
possibly Michal, his first and most proper wife, named last as given away to another

schools.summary: Abner 'made himself strong' = rendered powerful help; the house of Saul depended entirely on him, which sets up the coming rupture.
Wesley reads loyal exertion; Clarke reads a bid for the throne, since taking a royal concubine asserted a claim to the kingdom.
Gill simply marks the time-frame: the war lasted until the quarrel between Ish-bosheth and Abner broke it open.

sides.position: single-minded loyal support of Saul's house
an ambition already reaching toward the crown
